narrative:
High pressure in the Atlantic and a front stalled to our northwest combine to push warm, humid, unstable air into the Southern Appalachians. We have occasional showers and thundershowers today and into this evening. It's not an all-day rain, but any outdoor activity needs a radar check. Wednesday looks similar but will have less rain overall. A front pushes through from the north on Thursday, and shower and thundershower coverage will be even less — widely scattered.

The Memorial Day Monday Message is now available to subscribers. Drought relief is our top topic as the water faucet is back on!

2027 Blue Ridge Photo Winners Announced

The votes are in, and the winning photos have been selected. The Grand Prize Winner is a fall photo by Jason Frye of Price Lake looking toward Grandfather Mountain. See this and all the other winners at our Photo Contest Page.

Humid; Occasional Showers & Thundershowers

Last Updated Tue May 26 at 3:37PM by Scott Stephens

High pressure in the Atlantic and a front stalled to our northwest combine to push warm, humid, unstable air into the Southern Appalachians. We have occasional showers and thundershowers today and into this evening. It's not an all-day rain, but any outdoor activity needs a radar check. Wednesday looks similar but will have less rain overall. A front pushes through from the north on Thursday, and shower and thundershower coverage will be even less — widely scattered.

Forecast Discussion

Today's weather map has high pressure in the Atlantic and a front stalled to our northwest. Aiding and abetting the flow of warm, humid air into the Southern Appalachians is an upper-level disturbance in East Texas and Arkansas. We have periods of showers and thundershowers today and into this evening.

Wednesday, the upper-level disturbance fades. With that, shower and thundershower coverage will be less. Temperatures remain warm.

A pattern change begins Thursday. A surface front moves through from the north, propelled by a trough digging into the Northeast and eventually most of the Eastern U.S. Shower and thundershower coverage is reduced to widely scattered and mostly limited to the west side of the Appalachians.

Beginning Friday and through the rest of this 7-day forecast, the front will be stalled to our south with disturbances moving west to east along it. A secondary front moves through from the north late Saturday but does not change the storyline.

And the storyline is gradually less warm temperatures. We will have the potential of a shower or thundershower each day, with greater uncertainty late Friday night into Saturday as the most significant of these disturbances moves across the Southeast. Details about where the players line up on the field will determine how much rain reaches this far north. We do not know those details yet.

Detailed forecast discussion and analysis is only available to RaysWeather supporters.

Latest Insights
Monday Message for May 25, 2026

It's the Memorial Day Monday Message, where we recap our sorely-needed drought relief and discuss our region's cyclical warm/cool periods.

May 25, 2026
Rainy, Cool Saturday

It's a rainy, cool Saturday around western North Carolina and Southwest Virginia. High temperatures are running as much as 20-degrees below normal in the heart of the cold air damming zone.

Sunday and Memorial Day will continue to be unsettled, but temperatures will moderate and there will be additional breaks in the clouds, as the rainfall pattern becomes more convective, or showery in nature.

While the rainfall is falling on our holiday weekend, it's entirely beneficial with respect to our long-standing drought conditions.
